text: Laparoscopic gastro jejunostomy. Our patient was a seventy-one years old man with inoperable CS stomach with complete gastric outlet obstruction.

The surgeon retracts the colon and omentum superiorly to expose the duodenal jejunal flexure. The inferior mesenteric vein is visible, and upon further exposure, the duodenal jejunal flexure and inferior mesenteric artery are clearly seen.

The surgeon assesses the bowel to determine which segment can be freely brought to the anterior wall of the stomach. The stomach appears visibly dilated due to gastric outlet obstruction, indicating the intended site for anastomosis.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to make a gastrotomy incision in the thickened stomach wall, preparing for the jejunostomy procedure.

The surgeon widens the gastrotomy using medallion forceps and suctions the residue inside the stomach. The jejunum is brought up to the duodenal jejunal flexure, where the mesentery is opened to prepare for the anastomosis.

The surgeon creates an enterotomy in the jejunum using a monopolar hook, then widens it with Maryland forceps, exposing the mucosa. An endo-GIA stapler with a 60 mm blue cartridge is introduced for further anastomosis.

The surgeon applies the endo-GIA stapler first to the jejunal side and then to the gastric side, ensuring complete coverage. After locking the stapler, they assess the posterior aspect to confirm that no omentum or mesentery is encroaching on the staple line before firing the stapler.
The surgeon completes the anterior gastrojejunostomy, then assesses the staple line for any bleeding. Residue is suctioned out from the stomach due to the complete gastric outlet obstruction.
The surgeon uses a 2-0 PDS suture to place a stay suture on the right side of the enterotomy edge, preparing for the completion of the anastomosis.

The surgeon cuts the stay suture thread long and begins continuous closure from the left side of the enterotomy opening. The first suture bite is taken on the gastric side, followed by a reverse bite on the jejunal side, ensuring closure from stomach to jejunum. The surgeon then places the knot to secure the closure.

The surgeon secures the knot and continues the enterotomy closure, starting with the stomach side and then proceeding to the jejunum side. Full thickness bites are taken, including several reverse bites on the jejunal side, ensuring the mucosa does not protrude during tightening.

The surgeon continues the closure of the enterotomy, utilizing the stay suture to expose the angles for precise suturing. The stay suture aids in accessing the difficult angles, ensuring tight closure by taking bites one side at a time.

The surgeon reaches the edge of the closure, going beyond the stay suture, and places the final knot to complete the gastrojejunostomy. An air leak test is performed to assess the adequacy of the closure, marking the completion of the procedure.
